exercises.title: Prove the runtimes exist
exercises.prompt: Verify all three language runtimes from this lesson are installed and meet the minimum versions: Python 3.11+, Node 20+, and Rust (stable). Capture the version of each in a single command block.
exercises.hint: Each toolchain has a `--version` (or `-V`) flag. Chain them so one paste shows all three.
exercises.solution:
```bash
python3 --version   # Python 3.11.x or newer
node --version      # v20.x or newer
rustc --version     # rustc 1.7x.x (stable)
```

If any command is missing or below the minimum, reinstall that layer before moving on — every later lesson assumes these are present. On Windows, run these inside WSL2, not PowerShell.

exercises.title: A reproducible Python environment
exercises.prompt: Create an isolated Python environment for this course using `uv`, install `numpy`, and produce a lockfile-style record of exactly what's installed so a teammate could reproduce it. Then show that the package is importable only inside the environment.
exercises.hint: `uv venv` creates the environment; `uv pip install` adds packages; `uv pip freeze` prints exact pinned versions.
exercises.solution:
```bash
uv venv .venv                 # create the environment
source .venv/bin/activate     # (Windows WSL: same; PowerShell: .venv\Scripts\activate)
uv pip install numpy
uv pip freeze > requirements.txt   # exact, pinned versions
python -c "import numpy; print(numpy.__version__)"
```

`requirements.txt` now contains something like `numpy==2.1.3`. Deactivate with `deactivate` and rerun the import — it fails, proving the package lives in the environment, not globally. Reproduce elsewhere with `uv pip install -r requirements.txt`.

exercises.title: Detect the best available device
exercises.prompt: Write a small Python script that detects and prints the best available compute device — CUDA GPU, Apple MPS, or CPU — and runs one tensor operation on it to confirm it actually works. It should run unchanged on any of the three.
exercises.hint: PyTorch exposes `torch.cuda.is_available()` and `torch.backends.mps.is_available()`. Pick the device, move a tensor to it, do a matmul.
exercises.solution:
```python
import torch

if torch.cuda.is_available():
    device = "cuda"
elif torch.backends.mps.is_available():
    device = "mps"
else:
    device = "cpu"

print(f"Using device: {device}")
x = torch.randn(1000, 1000, device=device)
y = x @ x          # runs on the chosen device
print("ok", y.shape, y.device)
```

The `@` operator triggers a real matmul on the device; if the device were misconfigured this line would raise. Writing device-agnostic code like this from lesson one means the same scripts run on your laptop and a rented GPU without edits.
